content: The hybridisation and geometry of the complex $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ are
explanation:
$\mathrm{CN}^{-}$is a strong field ligand; it causes pairing in $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}\left(d^8\right)$.

The $d s p^2$ hybridisation forms a square planar geometry with no unpaired electrons.

content:
Match List-I with List-II for oxidation number of central metal atom.

$$ \begin{array}{llcc} \hline \begin{array}{c} \text { List-I } \\ (\text { Complex }) \end{array} & & \begin{array}{c} \text { List-II } \\ \text { (Oxidation number) } \end{array} \\ \hline \text { A. } \mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4 & \text { I. } & +1 \\ \hline \text { B. }\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}\right]^{2+} & \text { II. } & \text { Zero } \\ \hline \begin{array}{l} \text { C. }\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]^{2-} \\ (\mathrm{Each} \mathrm{Cr}) \end{array} & \text { III. } & -1 \\ \hline \begin{array}{l} \text { D. }\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CO})_{10}\right]^{2-} \\ (\mathrm{Each}) \end{array} & \text { IV. } & -2 \\ \hline \end{array} $$

Choose the correct match from the options given below
explanation:
The correct match is A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III.

A. $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$, all ligands neutral. So, oxidation state, $\mathrm{Ni}=0$

B. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}^{2+}, \mathrm{NO}^{+}=+1, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}=0\right.$. So oxidation state, $\mathrm{Fe}=+1$

C. $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]^{2-}, x+5(0)=-2$, So, oxidation state, $\mathrm{Co}=-2$

D. $\left[\mathrm{Cr}_2(\mathrm{CO})_{10}\right]^{2-}$, each $\mathrm{Cr}=-1($ since total $=-2)$

options.content: $ d s p^{2} $ and square planar

explanation:
$ \operatorname{In}\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_{4}\right]^{2-}, \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=3 d^{8} 4 s^{0} $

As $ \mathrm{CN}^{-} $is a strong ligand, pairing of electrons take place, Therefore,

$ d s p^{2} $ hybridisation gives square planar geometry.

content:
Match List I with List II.

List I
$($ Complex)		 List II
(Oxidation
number of metal)
A. $\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$ I. +1
B. $\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}\right]^{2+}$ II. Zero
C. $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]^{2-}$ III. -1
D. $\left[\mathrm{Cr}_2(\mathrm{CO})_{10}\right]^{2-}$ IV. -2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
explanation:
Let’s determine the oxidation number of the metal in each complex step by step.

For A. $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$

Each $$CO$$ ligand is neutral.

The overall complex is neutral.

Therefore, the oxidation number on Ni is $$0$$.

This matches List II item: II. Zero.

For B. $$\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}\right)_5 \mathrm{NO}\right]^{2+}$$

Water ($$H_2O$$) is neutral.

In many nitrosyl complexes, when the $$NO$$ ligand binds linearly, it is considered to act as $$NO^+$$ (nitrosonium).

If we assign $$+1$$ to the $$NO$$ ligand, let the oxidation state of Fe be $$x$$:

$$x + (+1)= +2$$

So, $$x = +1$$.

This matches List II item: I. +1.

For C. $$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CO})_5\right]^{2-}$$

Again, all carbonyl ($$CO$$) ligands are neutral.

The overall complex carries a charge of $$2-$$.

Hence, the oxidation number on Co is $$-2$$.

This matches List II item: IV. -2.

For D. $$\left[\mathrm{Cr}_2(\mathrm{CO})_{10}\right]^{2-}$$

There are two chromium atoms and all $$CO$$ ligands are neutral.

Let the oxidation number on each Cr be $$x$$. Since the overall charge is $$2-$$:

$$2x = -2$$

So, $$x = -1$$.

This matches List II item: III. -1.

Thus, the correct matching is:

A-II, B-I, C-IV, D-III

Among the options given, this corresponds to Option A.

content: The complex showing a spin-only magnetic moment of $$2.82 \mathrm{~BM}$$ is
explanation:
$$\mathrm{Ni}: 3 d^8, 4 s^2\quad$$ $$\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}: 3 d^8$$

Since, $$\mathrm{Cl}$$ is a weak field ligand, it doesn't cause pairing of electron.

Number of unpaired electrons, $$n=2$$

$$\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)}=\sqrt{2(2+2)} B M=\sqrt{8} ~B M=2.82 ~B M$$

content: The correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ is

options.content: nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.
have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.
have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

explanation:
To determine the correct statement with respect to the complexes $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, we need to analyze their coordination, oxidation states, and molecular geometries.

Option A: Nickel is in the same oxidation state in both.

To find the oxidation state of Ni in $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$, we consider that CO is a neutral ligand. Therefore, the oxidation state of Ni here is +0.

In $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$, since CN is a -1 ligand and the overall charge on the complex is -2, the oxidation state of Ni is +2.

So, nickel is not in the same oxidation state in both complexes.

Option B: Both have square planar geometry.

To determine the geometry, we need to consider the electronic configuration and the nature of the ligands:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: This complex has Ni in the 0 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8 4s^2$$. CO is a strong field ligand but with a $$d^10$$ configuration (Ni(0)), leading to a tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: This complex has Ni in the +2 oxidation state with an electronic configuration of $$3d^8$$. CN is a strong field ligand, causing a square planar geometry due to the dsp² hybridization to minimize repulsion.

Thus, both do not have square planar geometry.

Option C: Have square planar and tetrahedral geometry, respectively.

Based on our analysis of the electronic configuration and hybridization, it is clear that $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$ has a tetrahedral geometry and $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$ has a square planar geometry.

Hence, this statement is incorrect because it is the opposite of our findings.

Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

From the above analysis:

  • $$\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4$$: Tetrahedral geometry.
  • $$[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4]^{2-}$$: Square planar geometry.

This statement is correct based on our conclusions.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option D: Have tetrahedral and square planar geometry, respectively.

The electronic configuration of Cr is [Ar] 3d5 4s1.

[CrF6]3$$-$$ has d2sp3-hybridisation and octahedral geometry.

content: Select the correct statement about the complex [Co(NH3)5SO4] Br.

options.content: It gives yellow precipitate with AgNO<sub>3</sub>
Its ionisation isomer give while precipitate with BaCl<sub>2</sub>
All the above are correct statements.

content: Permanganate ion (MnO$$_4^ - $$) is dark purple coloured though Mn is in + 7 oxidation state with d0 configuration. This is due to

options.content: charge transfer from metal to ligand
charge transfer from ligand to metal

explanation:
In MnO$$_4^ - $$

x + ($$-$$2)4 = $$-$$1 $$\Rightarrow$$ x = + 7

$${}_{25}$$Mn+7 = [Ar], no unpaired elecrons.

Thus, it will now show d-d transition. It is dark purple coloured due to charge transfer from ligand to metal.

The oxidation state of Ni in [Ni(CO)4] is 0.

The electronic configuration of Ni is 3d8 4s2.

As CO is a strong ligand, pairing of electrons occur.

As, it has sp3-hybridisation, so the geometry is tetrahedral. Since, it has no unpaired electrons. So, the complex is diamgnetic.

content: Magnetic moment of in [Co(F6)]2$$-$$ of unpaired electron .......... .
explanation:
Oxidation number of Co,

x + ($$-$$1 $$\times$$ 6) = $$-$$2

$$\therefore$$ x = 4

$$\therefore$$ Electronic configuration of Co4+,

Co : [Ar] 3d5

$$\therefore$$ Number of unpaired electrons = 5

$$\therefore$$ Magnetic moment = $$\sqrt {n(n + 2)} = \sqrt {5 + 7} = \sqrt {35} $$ BM

content: According to IUPAC nomenclature sodium nitroprusside is named as
explanation: IUPAC name of sodiumnitroprusside Na2[Fe(CN)5NO] is sodiumpentacyanonitrosyl ferrate (III) because in it NO is neutral ligand and the oxidation number of Fe is III.

content: In qualitative analysis when H2S is passed through an aqueous solution of salt acidified with dilute HCl, a black ppt. is obtained. On boiling the precipitate with dil. HNO3, it forms a solution of blue colour. Addition of excess of aqueous solution of NH3 to this solution gives

options.content: deep blue solution of Cu(OH)<sub>2</sub> . Cu(NO<sub>3</sub>)<sub>2</sub>

explanation:
On passing H2S gas in the aqueous solution of salt in the presence of dilute HCl, black ppt of CuS is formed.

$$3CuS + 8HN{O_3} \to 3Cu{(N{O_3})_2} + 2NO + 3S + 4{H_2}O$$

$$C{u^{2 + }} + 4N{H_4}OH \to \mathop {{{[Cu{{(N{H_3})}_4}]}^{2 + }}}\limits_{Deep\,blue\,complex} + 4{H_2}O$$

content: Identify the incorrect statement regarding the complex [Cr(NH3)6]Cl3.

options.content: The complex involves d<sup>2</sup>sp<sup>3</sup> hybridisation and its octahedral in shape.
The complex is an outer orbital complex.
The complex gives white precipitate with silver nitrate solution.

explanation:
$$\underbrace {[Cr{{(N{H_3})}_6}]}_{Coordination\,sphere}\,\,\underbrace {C{l_3}}_{Ionisable}$$ $$\rightleftharpoons$$ $${[Cr{(N{H_3})_6}]^{3 + }} + 3C{l^ - }$$

$$[Cr{(N{H_3})_6}]C{l_3}\buildrel {AgN{O_3}} \over \longrightarrow \mathop {AgCl \downarrow }\limits_{(White\,ppt.)} $$

Config. of Cr (24) = 3d5 4s1

Config. Cr3+ = 3d3 4s0

Thus option (d) is correct.

(a) d2sp3 hybridisation, octahedral. Thus, option (a) is correct.

(b) here are three unpaired electrons, hence it is paramagnetic. Thus, option (b) is correct.

(c) d2sp3 inner orbital complex. Thus option (c) is as incorrect.

(d) Due to ionisable Cl$$-$$ ions, white ppt with AgNO3. Thus, option (d) is correct.

So, the incorrect statement is (c).
